The Solow Model Unleashed

LT5 | EMBA2027 | Brizuela, Magbitang, Pascua, Valencia

Solow Growth Model Simulation

Transition dynamics, steady state, and why France's growth slowed after the 1970s
Y = A · Kα · L1−α
Production Function — output from technology, capital & labor
|
Δk̃ = s · f(k̃) − (n + g + δ)k̃
Capital Accumulation — investment minus break-even
Parameters
Savings Rate (s)22%
Depreciation (δ)4.4%
Population Growth (n)1.0%
Technology Growth (g)2.5%
Capital Share (α)0.30
Initial TFP (A0)0.99
Initial k̃ (capital/eff. worker)4.0
Labor Participation (N/POP)1.25
Case Presets
Philippines Scenarios
Steady State
--
k̃*
--
ỹ*
--
SS Growth
--
Yrs to 90%

GDP per Capita Path

Red = actual path from initial conditions. Gold dashed = steady-state path. The gap shows how far the economy is from long-run equilibrium.

Capital per Effective Worker (k̃)

Red = k̃ over time. Gold dashed = k̃* (steady state). Capital rises when investment exceeds break-even, then levels off.

Solow Diagram

Green curve = s·f(k̃), concave due to diminishing returns. Red line = (n+g+δ)·k̃, break-even investment. Where they cross = k̃*. Gold dot = current position.

GDP per Capita Growth Rate (%)

Red = growth rate over time. Gold dashed = steady-state growth (= g). Growth starts high when far from steady state, then decays toward g. Only technology drives permanent growth.

Growth Accounting Decomposition

Using Exhibit 2 from the case: Y/POP = A · (K/N)α · (N/POP) -- decomposing the France/US gap
France's GDP per capita went from 57% of the US (1950) to 88% (1980) then back to 79% (2000). The decomposition reveals TFP catch-up was the dominant driver of convergence, while declining labor utilization caused the reversal. Capital intensity actually exceeded the US by 1980 and stayed above it.

France/US Ratios (PWT 11.0)

YearY/POPA (TFP)(K/N)^0.3N/POP
19500.570.480.891.32
19800.880.881.130.88
20000.790.941.200.70
Verification: 0.48 × 0.89 × 1.32 = 0.56 ≈ 0.57 ✓
0.88 × 1.13 × 0.88 = 0.88 ✓
0.94 × 1.20 × 0.70 = 0.79 ✓

Decomposition of France/US GDP Gap

1950: Why France was at 57%

Post-war France had very low TFP (0.48) -- the dominant source of the gap. Capital was moderately below the US ((K/N)^0.3 = 0.89). But France had higher labor utilization (1.32) -- everyone was working to rebuild.

1980: Peak Convergence at 88%

TFP nearly closed the gap (0.48 → 0.88). Capital exceeded the US (1.13). But labor utilization already started declining (1.32 → 0.88). This is when Grandpa Frank should have recognized the slowdown.

2000: Reversal to 79%

Capital well above the US ((K/N)^0.3 = 1.20). TFP nearly equal (0.94). The entire gap is labor utilization: N/POP = 0.70. 35-hour work week, 5-week vacation, high unemployment (11%).

Growth Rate Decomposition Over Time

Game Theory: China Market Entry Decision

Applying strategic analysis to Durabuild's decision whether to expand into China -- the question Lisa faces at the end of the case
Lisa must decide: does China present long-term potential, or will its growth phase peter out like France? The case notes China's extraordinarily high investment rate, difficult regulatory environment, and questions about technology adoption and the one-child policy's demographic effects.

1. Decision Under Uncertainty

Durabuild chooses Enter or Stay Out. Nature determines if China sustains high growth or slows (like France did). Adjust the probability and payoffs to see how the expected value changes.

P(High Growth Continues)60%
Payoff: Enter + High Growth150
Payoff: Enter + Slowdown-80
Payoff: Stay Out0
EXPECTED VALUES
E[Enter] = 58.0
E[Stay Out] = 0.0
Optimal: ENTER
BREAK-EVEN PROBABILITY
--
Minimum P(High Growth) needed for entry to be rational

2. Strategic Entry Game (2-Player)

Durabuild and a Competitor simultaneously decide whether to enter China. Payoffs depend on market crowding. Green highlight = Nash Equilibrium.

Both Enter (each)30
Only You Enter120
Only Rival Enters (you stay)-10
Neither Enters0
Competitor: EnterCompetitor: Stay Out
Durabuild: Enter 30, 30 120, -10
Durabuild: Stay Out -10, 120 0, 0

3. Sequential Decision Tree

If Durabuild moves first (invest in China assessment), then decides to enter or not based on findings.

4. Mixed Strategy Equilibrium

If the entry game has no pure-strategy NE, what probability should each player enter with?

5. Sensitivity: Entry EV vs Probability of High Growth

How the expected value of entering China changes as you vary P(growth continues). The vertical line marks the break-even probability.

Convergence Analysis

The Solow model predicts conditional convergence: countries with similar fundamentals converge to the same steady state. Absolute convergence is not guaranteed.
The case demonstrates conditional convergence perfectly: France converged to the US in capital intensity (K/N ratio matched by 2000) but NOT in GDP per capita -- because they had different labor market institutions (the "conditions" diverged).

Comparative GDP per Capita (Index = 100 at Year 0)

Growth Rate Convergence

Poorer countries grow faster initially (higher marginal product of capital) then slow toward steady-state growth rate g.

Capital-to-Labor Ratio Convergence

France started below the US capital-to-labor ratio in 1950 and surpassed it by 1980. The Solow model predicts this: with similar savings rates, capital per worker converges regardless of starting point.

Why France Never Fully Caught Up: Conditional vs Absolute Convergence

1.20
(K/N)^0.3 ratio in 2000
Capital: EXCEEDED US
0.94
TFP ratio in 2000
Technology: NEARLY CONVERGED
0.70
N/POP ratio in 2000
Labor: DIVERGED
The Solow model predicts convergence in capital and technology -- and it happened. But the model also tells us that steady-state output depends on labor inputs, which are shaped by institutional factors: the 35-hour work week, mandatory 5-week vacation, 60% wage replacement unemployment insurance, high marginal tax rates, strict firing regulations, and high minimum wage. These are the "conditions" that make France's steady state lower than the US's.

Philippines in the Convergence Framework

~0.14
PH/US GDP per capita (PPP)
$11,794 vs ~$85,000 (World Bank 2024)
~0.35
Relative TFP (est.)
BSP DP 2024-10: post-pandemic recovery
0.64
Labor Force Participation
CEIC/PSA LFS, Q3 2025
The Philippines presents a different convergence puzzle than France. Where France converged in capital but diverged in labor, the PH is far from convergence on all fronts: low capital stock, low TFP, and moderate labor participation. The Solow model predicts this should mean high growth potential -- the marginal product of capital is high when k is low. But three structural issues suppress the transition: (1) gross capital formation is only ~23% of GDP (World Bank 2023), well below China's 43% or Vietnam's 33%; (2) TFP growth was historically negative for most of 1967-2000 (Cororaton 2005, Asian Development Review), only turning mildly positive after 1990s reforms, and recovering post-pandemic but still below pre-2019 trends (BSP DP 2024-10); (3) brain drain through OFW migration exports productive workers -- remittances reached $38.3B in 2024 (8.3% of GDP, BSP) but flow primarily to household consumption rather than investment.
The key insight: the Philippines has the initial conditions for catch-up growth (low k, high MPK), but not yet the structural conditions (savings, TFP growth, human capital retention) to realize it. Note also that population growth has slowed sharply to 0.8% annually (PSA 2024 Census, down from 1.63% in 2015-2020), which mechanically raises k* -- fewer workers to share capital across -- potentially aiding convergence if sustained.

Policy Analysis & China Application

How changes in savings, labor policy, and technology adoption affect long-run GDP -- applying lessons from France to evaluate China
Lisa's key question: will China's high growth persist? The Solow model gives a clear framework. Growth from capital accumulation is temporary (transitional). Only TFP growth is permanent. China's 45% savings rate drives fast transition, but the steady state depends on g, n, and institutional quality.

Policy Lever: Savings Rate

Higher s raises the steady-state k* and y*, but has diminishing returns due to α < 1. China's s=45% is historically unprecedented.

Policy Lever: Labor Participation

GDP per capita = A · f(k) · (N/POP). Labor participation is a direct multiplier. France lost 28% of its GDP gap to the US from labor alone.

China Scenario Builder

Adjust China-specific parameters to project different outcomes for Durabuild's potential market.
China Savings Rate45%
China TFP Growth3.5%
China Pop Growth0.5%
Regulatory Friction0.85
Technology Adoption0.70
One-Child Pop Effect (yrs)15
--
China Projected k*
--
China Projected y*
--
Peak Growth Rate

References & Penn World Table Guide

Data sources, references, and how to use PWT 11.0 to solve the Durabuild case
The case's Exhibits 1 & 2 use Penn World Tables 7.1 (per Exhibit 1 source note). Students should replicate and extend the analysis using PWT 11.0 (released October 7, 2025), which covers 185 countries through 2023 and incorporates the 2021 ICP purchasing power parity benchmarks.

Calibration Methodology

Preset parameters fall into three categories:

Data (directly observed): s (investment/GDP from World Bank WDI or PWT csh_i), n (population growth from national census data), N/POP (labor force participation from case Exhibit 2 or national labor force surveys).

Estimated: g (TFP growth) is always a Solow residual — not directly measured — and published estimates vary widely for any country/period. A0 for France and the US comes from case Exhibit 2; for China and the Philippines it is backed out from GDP per capita PPP ratios. α = 0.30 is the textbook convention (Mankiw, Romer & Weil 1992); PWT labsh data suggests 0.35–0.40.

Calibrated: k̃0 (initial capital per effective worker) is a model-normalized variable (K/(AL)) that cannot be directly observed as a single number. It is set so that each preset produces plausible initial growth dynamics consistent with historical GDP growth rates for the period.

The three PH counterfactual scenarios (Brain Drain, Demographic Dividend, High Investment) are thought experiments. Their modified parameters are hypothetical, not forecasts.

Philippines Data Sources

n = 0.8%: PSA 2024 Census of Population (Proclamation 973), annualized growth 2020–2024.

s = 23%: World Bank WDI, Gross Capital Formation 23.3% of GDP (2023).

LFPR = 64%: CEIC / PSA Labour Force Survey, 63.6% (Q3 2025).

δ = 5%: Standard developing-economy depreciation rate (PWT convention; simulation uses 4.4% for developed economies).

A0 = 0.35: Estimated from PH/US GDP per capita PPP ratio (~14%; $11,794 vs ~$85,000, World Bank 2024); backed out from production function.

g = 1.0%: BSP Discussion Paper 2024-10 (Amodia et al.): TFP growth recovering post-pandemic but below pre-2019 trend of ~1.5%.

g = 0.3% (Brain Drain): Cororaton (2005, Asian Development Review): TFP growth was negative for most of 1967–2000, averaging −0.5% to +0.4%.

OFW remittances: $38.3B = 8.3% of GDP (BSP, as reported by PSA for 2024).

GDP growth: 5.7% (World Bank, 2024 actual).

Population: 112.7M (PSA 2024 POPCEN).

Penn World Table 11.0

PWT 11.0 is the latest version, published October 7, 2025 by the Groningen Growth and Development Centre (GGDC), University of Groningen. It is a major update from PWT 10.01, extending coverage to 185 countries through 2023 with new 2021 ICP PPP benchmarks.

Access the data:

https://www.rug.nl/ggdc/productivity/pwt/

FRED: fred.stlouisfed.org/release?rid=285

Query tool: pwt-data-tool.streamlit.app

Required citation:

Feenstra, Robert C., Robert Inklaar and Marcel P. Timmer (2015), "The Next Generation of the Penn World Table," American Economic Review, 105(10), 3150-3182.

What changed from PWT 7.1 (used in the case)?

PWT 11.0 incorporates revised 2017 and 2021 ICP PPP benchmarks, extends data to 2023, uses official China national accounts (replacing the Maddison/Wu adjusted series), and adds refined TFP series. Results for France and the US differ significantly from the case's Exhibit 2 values (which used PWT 7.1). The decomposition data shown on this site uses PWT 11.0.

Key PWT Variables for This Case

VariableDescriptionUse in Solow
rgdpnaReal GDP at constant national prices (mil. 2017 US$)Y -- compute y = rgdpna/emp
rnnaCapital stock at constant national prices (mil. 2017 US$)K -- compute k = rnna/emp
empNumber of persons engaged (millions)L -- labor input
avhAverage annual hours worked per workerN -- total hours = emp * avh
popPopulation (millions)POP -- for Y/POP, N/POP
labshShare of labour compensation in GDP1 - α (so α = 1 - labsh)
rtfpnaTFP at constant national prices (2017=1)A -- total factor productivity index
ctfpTFP level at current PPPs (USA=1)A -- cross-country TFP comparison
deltaAverage depreciation rate of capital stockδ -- directly usable
csh_iShare of gross capital formation in GDP (expenditure)s -- investment/savings rate
hcHuman capital index (based on years of schooling + returns)Augmented Solow (MRW 1992)

How to Replicate Exhibit 2 Using PWT 11.0

The case decomposes France/US GDP per capita as: Y/POP = A · (K/N)α · (N/POP)

Step 1: Extract data for France (FRA) and the United States (USA)

Download PWT 11.0. Filter for country codes FRA and USA. Select years of interest (1950, 1980, 2000, and extend to 2019 or 2023).

Step 2: Compute total hours per capita (N/POP)

N / POP = (emp × avh) / pop

Then compute the France/US ratio: (N/POP)FRA / (N/POP)USA

Step 3: Compute capital-to-labor ratio

K / N = rnna / (emp × avh)

Then compute (K/N)FRA/(K/N)USA and raise to the power α. The standard value is α = 0.3; alternatively use α = 1 - labsh from PWT directly.

Step 4: Back out TFP (A) as a residual

A = (Y / POP) / [(K / N)α · (N / POP)]

Or use the ctfp variable directly for the cross-country TFP level comparison (USA=1).

Step 5: Verify the decomposition

(Y / POP)ratio = Aratio · (K / N)αratio · (N / POP)ratio

The product of the three right-hand terms should equal the left-hand term. This is an accounting identity -- it must hold.

Step 6: Extend to the Philippines (PHL) and China (CHN)

Repeat the same decomposition for PHL/USA and CHN/USA ratios. This lets you apply the case's analytical framework beyond France and answer Lisa's question about China -- and apply it to the Philippine context for class discussion.

Growth Accounting with PWT (Solow Residual Method)

To decompose growth rates over time (not just level differences), use the growth accounting equation:

gY/L = α · gK/L + gA     (Solow residual: gA = gY/L − α · gK/L)

Using PWT variables:

y = log(rgdpna / emp)    # log GDP per worker
k = log(rnna / emp)      # log capital per worker
α = 1 - labsh            # capital share
g_y = Δy                  # growth of output per worker
g_k = Δk                  # growth of capital per worker
g_A = g_y - α * g_k       # TFP growth (Solow residual)

This is the method used in Solow (1957). The R code walkthrough using PWT is available at r-econometrics.com.

Cited in the Case

Labor Market & French Slowdown (Endnote 1)

Solow, R.M. (2000). "Unemployment in the United States and in Europe: A Contrast and the Reasons." Working Paper no. 231.

Blanchard, O.J. (2004). "Explaining European Unemployment." NBER Reporter: Research Summary.

Rogerson, R. (2006). "Understanding Differences in Hours Worked." Review of Economic Dynamics, 9(3).

Game Theory Foundations

Von Neumann, J. and O. Morgenstern (1944). Theory of Games and Economic Behavior. Princeton University Press.

Nash, J. (1950). "Equilibrium Points in N-Person Games." PNAS, 36(1), 48-49.

Selten, R. (1965). "Spieltheoretische Behandlung eines Oligopolmodells mit Nachfragetragheit." Zeitschrift fur die Gesamte Staatswissenschaft, 121, 301-324.

Philippines TFP

Amodia, R.A. et al. (2024). "Estimating the Philippines' Total Factor Productivity Growth." BSP Discussion Paper No. 2024-10.

Cororaton, C.B. (2005). "Total Factor Productivity Growth in the Philippines." Asian Development Review, 22(1), 97-113.

Stylized Facts

Kaldor, N. (1961). "Capital Accumulation and Economic Growth." In F.A. Lutz and D.C. Hague (eds.), The Theory of Capital.
